docs.title: New York Restaurants
docs.text: Technical writers who understand data are better positioned to document the systems that produce it. The examples in this section demonstrate a working knowledge of SQL — from querying relational datasets to surfacing trends through structured analysis. These pieces are intended to show how data literacy can inform documentation strategy, support evidence-based decision making, and enable more meaningful collaboration with engineering and analytics teams.

docs.title: New York Restaurants — SQL Analysis Project
Introduction
docs.text:
This project demonstrates the use of SQL to explore and analyse a fictional dataset of New York City restaurants. Using a series of structured queries, I examined the data, filtered restaurants based on different criteria, ranked results, and transformed numeric values into meaningful categories.

The aim of this project is to showcase practical SQL skills commonly used in data analysis and real-world problem solving.

docs.title: Project Overview
docs.text:
The dataset is stored in a table called nomnom, which contains information about restaurant names, locations, cuisines, review scores, pricing, and health grades.

Throughout this analysis, I used SQL to:

  • Explore the structure of the dataset
  • Identify patterns and categories within the data
  • Filter results based on realistic scenarios
  • Rank and classify restaurants using conditional logic

docs.title: Column Names
docs.text:
The dataset includes the following fields:

  • 'name'
  • 'neighborhood'
  • 'cuisine'
  • 'review'
  • 'price'
  • 'health'

docs.title: Distinct Neighborhoods
docs.text:
To identify which neighborhoods are represented in the dataset, I queried the unique values in the neighborhood column:

SELECT DISTINCT neighborhood
FROM nomnom;

Neighborhoods identified: - Brooklyn - Midtown - Chinatown - Uptown - Queens - Downtown

docs.title: Distinct Cuisine Types
docs.text:
To understand the range of cuisines available, I queried the distinct cuisine values:

SELECT DISTINCT cuisine
FROM nomnom;

docs.title: Cuisine types included:
docs.text:
• Steak
  • Korean
  • Chinese
  • Pizza
  • Ethiopian
  • Vegetarian
  • Italian
  • Japanese
  • American
  • Mediterranean
  • Indian
  • Soul Food
  • Mexican

docs.title: Chinese Takeout Options
docs.text:
To find all restaurants offering Chinese cuisine:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE cuisine = 'Chinese';

This query filters the dataset using a categorical condition.

docs.title: Restaurants with High Review Scores
docs.text:
To identify restaurants with review scores of 4 and above:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE review >= 4;

This demonstrates numeric filtering based on customer ratings.

docs.title: Italian Restaurants for a Fancy Dinner
docs.text:
To find Italian restaurants priced at exactly three dollar signs:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE cuisine = 'Italian'
  AND price = '$$$';

To include Italian restaurants priced at at least three dollar signs:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE cuisine = 'Italian'
  AND price LIKE '%$$$%';

These queries demonstrate the use of logical operators and pattern matching.

docs.title: Searching with Wildcards
docs.text:
If the name of a restaurant is only partially remembered (for example, containing the word “meatball”), wildcard searching can be used:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE name LIKE '%meatball%';

The % symbol allows flexible pattern matching within text fields.

docs.title: Delivery-Friendly Neighborhoods
docs.text:
To find restaurants located in Midtown, Downtown, or Chinatown:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE neighborhood = 'Midtown'
   OR neighborhood = 'Downtown'
   OR neighborhood = 'Chinatown';

This query combines multiple conditions using the OR operator.

docs.title: Restaurants with Pending Health Grades
docs.text:
Some restaurants do not yet have a recorded health grade. These missing values are represented as NULL.

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
WHERE health IS NULL;

This query demonstrates how to identify incomplete data.

docs.title: Top 10 Restaurants by Review Score
docs.text:
To rank restaurants from highest to lowest review score:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
ORDER BY review DESC;

To limit the results to the top 10 restaurants:

SELECT *
FROM nomnom
ORDER BY review DESC
LIMIT 10;

This approach is commonly used to identify top-performing entries in a dataset.

docs.title: Transforming Data with CASE Statements
docs.text:
To convert numeric review scores into descriptive categories, I used a CASE statement:

SELECT name,
CASE
  WHEN review > 4.5 THEN 'Extraordinary'
  WHEN review > 4 THEN 'Excellent'
  WHEN review > 3 THEN 'Good'
  WHEN review > 2 THEN 'Fair'
  ELSE 'Poor'
END AS Review
FROM nomnom;

This transformation improves readability and makes the data more meaningful to non-technical audiences.

docs.title: Skills Demonstrated
docs.text:
• SQL querying (SELECT, WHERE)
  • Data exploration (DISTINCT)
  • Logical operators (AND, OR)
  • Pattern matching (LIKE)
  • Handling missing values (IS NULL)
  • Sorting and ranking (ORDER BY, LIMIT)
  • Conditional logic (CASE)

docs.title: SQLite
docs.text: Technical writers who understand data are better positioned to document the systems that produce it. The examples in this section demonstrate a working knowledge of SQL — from querying relational datasets to surfacing trends through structured analysis. These pieces are intended to show how data literacy can inform documentation strategy, support evidence-based decision making, and enable more meaningful collaboration with engineering and analytics teams.

docs.title: SQLite — Installation, Usage, and Data Analysis
Introduction
docs.text:
This project documents my exploration of SQLite, a lightweight, file-based relational database engine. The aim was to understand how SQLite works, how it differs from server-based databases, and how to install, configure, and query it locally using real-world data.

The project combines: - Conceptual understanding of SQLite - Hands-on installation and configuration - Debugging common beginner errors - SQL analysis using a public dataset

docs.title: What is SQLite?
docs.text:
SQLite is a database engine that allows users to interact with a relational database stored in a single file. Unlike systems such as PostgreSQL or MySQL, SQLite does not require a separate server process.

This design makes SQLite: - Extremely portable - Easy to copy and share - Ideal for local development, testing, and embedded systems

A database can be moved or backed up simply by copying the file that stores it.

docs.title: Drawbacks of SQLite
docs.text:
While SQLite is highly accessible, it has limitations:

  • Only one user can write to the database at a time
  • Security must be managed carefully due to file-based access
  • Advanced database features are limited compared to full server systems
  • SQLite does not strictly enforce data types

For example, SQLite allows values of any type to be inserted into any column, even when a schema defines expected types:

CREATE TABLE celebs (
  id INTEGER,
  name TEXT,
  age INTEGER
);

docs.title: Common Use Cases for SQLite
docs.text: Despite its drawbacks, SQLite is widely used for: - Local application development - Testing and prototyping - Embedded systems - Applications where the database lives alongside the code SQLite is considered one of the most widely deployed software components in the world.

docs.title: Step 1: Locate SQLite Tools
docs.text:
After downloading the SQLite tools, I navigated through the filesystem:

ls
cd Desktop
ls
cd sqlite-tools-osx-x64-3510100

This confirmed the location of the SQLite binaries.

docs.title: Step 2: Move sqlite3 into the System PATH
docs.text:
Initial attempt:

mv sqlite3 /usr/local/bin/

Result:

Permission denied

Corrected attempt using administrator privileges:

sudo mv sqlite3 /usr/local/bin/sqlite3

This succeeded because /usr/local/bin is a protected directory.

docs.title: Step 3: Verify SQLite Installation
docs.text:
sqlite3 --version

Output:

SQLite 3.43.2 2023-10-10 (64-bit)

This confirmed:

  • SQLite was installed correctly
  • The binary was accessible globally
  • The interactive SQLite shell could launch

docs.title: Step 4: Learn the difference between Terminal vs SQLite Shell
docs.text:
I learned the important distinction between:

  • Terminal shell (zsh)
  • SQLite shell (sqlite>)

Attempting shell commands inside SQLite:

sqlite> pwd
sqlite> ls

Result:

  • SQLite entered continuation mode (...>)

Exit safely with:

Ctrl + C

Key learning:

  • Shell commands do not work inside SQLite.
  • SQLite expects valid SQL.
  • Ctrl + C safely cancels unfinished SQL

docs.title: Step 5: Opening the Database (First Mistake)
docs.text:
Attempt:

sqlite3 acs-1-year-2015.sqlite

Inside SQLite:

.schema

Result:

  • No output

What happened:

SQLite silently created a new empty database because the file did not exist in the current directory.

docs.title: Step 6: Locating the Correct Database File
docs.text:
To find the actual database:

find ~ -name "acs-1-year-2015.sqlite" 2>/dev/null

Result:

/Users/username/Desktop/acs-1-year-2015.sqlite
/Users/username/acs-1-year-2015.sqlite

Key insight:

SQLite creates a new database file if the specified file does not exist.

docs.title: Step 7: Opening the Correct Database
docs.text: sqlite3 /Users/username/Desktop/acs-1-year-2015.sqlite

docs.title: Step 8: Inspecting Database Contents
docs.text:
.databases

Output:

main: /Users/username/Desktop/acs-1-year-2015.sqlite r/w

.tables

Output:

congressional_districts
places
states

Then:

.schema

Result:

  • Full schemas for all tables
  • Indexes on key columns for performance

docs.title: Error 1: Column Name Typo
docs.text:
• per_captia_income ❌
  • per_capita_income ✅

SQLite returned: Parse error: no such column

SELECT per_captia_income FROM states;

Error:

Parse error: no such column

Correct column name:

per_capita_income

docs.title: Error 2: Multiple SELECT Statements
docs.text:
Typing multiple SELECT clauses caused:

Parse error: near "SELECT": syntax error

Fix:

  • Press Ctrl + C
  • Re-enter a single valid SQL statement

docs.title: Per Capita Income by State
docs.text:
SELECT name, per_capita_income
FROM states
ORDER BY per_capita_income ASC;

Result (excerpt):

Puerto Rico|18626 Mississippi|40593 Arkansas|41995 West Virginia|42019 ... California|64500 Massachusetts|70628 New Jersey|72222 Hawaii|73486 District of Columbia|75628 Maryland|75847

Insight:

  • Significant income disparities across states
  • Southern states clustered at the lower end
  • Coastal and urban regions ranked highest

docs.title: Median Age by State
docs.text:
SELECT name, median_age
FROM states
ORDER BY median_age ASC;

docs.title: Result (excerpt):
docs.text:
Utah|30.6 Alaska|33.3 District of Columbia|33.8 Texas|34.4 ... Florida|41.8 West Virginia|42.2 New Hampshire|42.8 Vermont|43.1 Maine|44.6

Insight:

  • Utah has the youngest population
  • Northeastern states skew older
  • Florida’s age profile reflects retirement migration

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Installed and verified SQLite on macOS
  • Understood the difference between Terminal and SQLite shell
  • Learned how SQLite handles missing database files
  • Explored databases using .databases, .tables, and .schema
  • Debugged real SQL errors
  • Analysed demographic and economic data using SQL
  • Interpreted results in a real-world context

docs.title: Trends in Startups
docs.text: Technical writers who understand data are better positioned to document the systems that produce it. The examples in this section demonstrate a working knowledge of SQL — from querying relational datasets to surfacing trends through structured analysis. These pieces are intended to show how data literacy can inform documentation strategy, support evidence-based decision making, and enable more meaningful collaboration with engineering and analytics teams.

docs.title: How to Analyze Startup Trends Using SQL
docs.text: This guide walks you step by step through analyzing startup data using SQL. By the end, you’ll understand how to explore a dataset, use aggregate functions, group data, and extract meaningful business insights.

docs.title: What You’ll Be Working With
docs.text:
You are given a SQLite database containing a table called startups. This table represents a portfolio of well-known startups and includes information such as valuation, funding stage, location, and number of employees.

Your goal is to ask useful questions of the data and answer them using SQL.

docs.title: Step 1: Explore the Data Structure
docs.text:
Before doing any analysis, you need to understand what the table looks like.

SELECT *
FROM startups;

This query displays all rows and columns, helping you understand what data is available.

docs.title: Step 2: Count the Number of Startups
docs.text:
SELECT COUNT(*)
FROM startups;

This query counts the total number of startups in the table.

docs.title: Step 3: Calculate the Total Market Value
docs.text:
SELECT SUM(valuation)
FROM startups;

This calculates the combined valuation of all startups in the dataset.

docs.title: Step 4: Find the Largest Funding Round
docs.text:
SELECT MAX(raised)
FROM startups;

This returns the highest funding amount raised by any startup.

docs.title: Step 5: Filter by Startup Stage
docs.text:
SELECT MAX(raised)
FROM startups
WHERE stage = 'Seed';

This limits the analysis to Seed-stage startups only.

docs.title: Step 6: Identify the Oldest Startup
docs.text:
SELECT MIN(founded)
FROM startups;

This returns the earliest founding year in the dataset.

docs.title: Step 7: Calculate the Average Valuation
docs.text:
SELECT AVG(valuation)
FROM startups;

This provides a baseline average valuation across all startups.

docs.title: Step 8: Compare Valuation by Category
docs.text:
SELECT category, AVG(valuation)
FROM startups
GROUP BY category;

This groups startups by category and calculates the average valuation for each.

docs.title: Step 9: Round Results for Readability
docs.text:
SELECT category, ROUND(AVG(valuation), 2)
FROM startups
GROUP BY category;

This rounds average valuations to two decimal places.

docs.title: Step 10: Rank Categories by Valuation
docs.text:
SELECT category, ROUND(AVG(valuation), 2)
FROM startups
GROUP BY 1
ORDER BY 2 DESC;

This sorts categories from highest to lowest average valuation.

docs.title: Step 11: Identify Competitive Markets
docs.text:
SELECT category, COUNT(*)
FROM startups
GROUP BY category;

This shows how many startups exist in each category.

docs.title: Step 12: Filter Competitive Categories
docs.text:
SELECT category, COUNT(*)
FROM startups
GROUP BY category
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
ORDER BY 2 DESC;

This keeps only categories with more than three startups.

docs.title: Step 13: Analyze Startup Size by Location
docs.text:
SELECT location, AVG(employees)
FROM startups
GROUP BY location;

This calculates average startup size by location.

docs.title: Step 14: Focus on Large Startup Hubs
docs.text:
SELECT location, AVG(employees)
FROM startups
GROUP BY location
HAVING AVG(employees) > 500;

This highlights locations with large average team sizes.

docs.title: Key Takeaways
docs.text:
• Always explore the data first
  • Use aggregate functions to summarize information
  • Use GROUP BY to compare categories or locations
  • Use HAVING to filter aggregated results
  • Order results to highlight trends

This workflow applies to almost any SQL-based data analysis task.
